vintage.tax: Effective property tax rate on owner-occupied housing, calendar year 2023 (Tax Foundation analysis of Census ACS data, published 2025)
vintage.home: Typical home value, Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI), 2025, rounded to the nearest $5,000
vintage.ins: Average annual homeowners insurance premium for $300k dwelling coverage (Bankrate/Quadrant 2025), rounded to the nearest $50

sources: Tax Foundation, 'Property Taxes by State and County' (2023 data)
U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (underlying tax/value data)
Bankrate, 'Average cost of homeowners insurance' (2025, Quadrant Information Services data)
State revenue department publications (transfer taxes, homestead rules)
meta.caveat: State-level figures are planning defaults, not quotes: property taxes vary by county and city, insurance varies by home and insurer, and home values move monthly. Every value prefills the calculator but remains editable.
meta.verified: Spot-verified 2026-07-11 vs published sources: property-tax rates for NJ/IL/TX/HI/AL/NV/CO/SC/CT (Tax Foundation 2023); insurance for NE/LA/FL/HI/AL (Bankrate 2025 $300k dwelling; OK approximated from its top-3 ranking); home values for NJ/HI (Zillow). Remaining values are from the same published series; all values are editable in the calculator and flagged to verify locally.

states.name: Alabama
states.transfer: Deed tax of $0.50 per $500 of value (0.1%) — among the lowest in the country.
states.taxNote: Alabama has the second-lowest effective property tax rate in the U.S.; homeowners 65+ are exempt from the state portion.
states.insNote: Gulf-adjacent counties pay materially more for wind coverage.

states.name: Alaska
states.transfer: No state transfer tax on real estate deeds.
states.taxNote: Only about half of Alaska's boroughs levy property taxes — rates vary enormously by borough.

states.name: Arizona
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax (a flat $2 deed recording fee applies).
states.taxNote: Assessed value growth is capped at 5% per year for primary residences (Prop 117).

states.name: Arkansas
states.transfer: Real property transfer tax of $3.30 per $1,000 (0.33%) on sales above $100.
states.insNote: Hail and tornado exposure keeps premiums well above the national average.

states.name: California
states.transfer: Documentary transfer tax of $1.10 per $1,000 (0.11%); some cities add much more.
states.taxNote: Prop 13 caps the base tax at 1% of purchase price with assessed-value growth limited to 2%/year — long-time owners often pay far below the market-value rate.
states.insNote: Wildfire-zone homes face sharply higher premiums or state FAIR Plan coverage.

states.name: Colorado
states.transfer: Documentary fee of $0.02 per $100 (0.02%) — nominal.
states.insNote: Hail is the big premium driver — Front Range roofs are among the most expensive to insure in the country.

states.name: Connecticut
states.transfer: State conveyance tax of 0.75%–2.25% by price tier, plus 0.25% municipal.
states.taxNote: Third-highest effective property tax rate in the nation — factor it into any affordability math.

states.name: Delaware
states.transfer: 4% transfer tax (typically split buyer/seller) — one of the highest in the U.S.
states.taxNote: Low annual property taxes but the steep one-time transfer tax changes closing-cost math.

states.name: Florida
states.transfer: Documentary stamp tax of $0.70 per $100 (0.7%; Miami-Dade differs).
states.taxNote: The homestead exemption removes up to $50,000 of assessed value and the Save Our Homes cap limits assessment growth to 3%/year for primary residences.
states.insNote: Hurricane exposure makes Florida one of the most expensive states to insure a home; many owners carry separate windstorm and flood policies.

states.name: Georgia
states.transfer: Transfer tax of $1 per $1,000 (0.1%).

states.name: Hawaii
states.transfer: Conveyance tax from 0.1% to 1.25%, rising with price and higher for non-residents.
states.taxNote: The lowest property tax rate in the nation — but applied to the highest home values, so dollar bills are still substantial.
states.insNote: Standard policies commonly exclude hurricane — a separate hurricane policy is typical.

states.name: Idaho
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.

states.name: Illinois
states.transfer: State transfer tax of $0.50 per $500 (0.1%) plus county $0.25 per $500; Chicago adds much more.
states.taxNote: Second-highest effective property tax rate in the U.S. — on the median home it rivals the principal-and-interest payment's escrow share.

states.name: Indiana
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.
states.taxNote: Property tax is capped at 1% of gross assessed value for homesteads by the state constitution.

states.name: Iowa
states.transfer: Real estate transfer tax of $0.80 per $500 (0.16%) above $500.
states.insNote: Hail and windstorm claims keep Midwest premiums elevated.

states.name: Kansas
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax (mortgage registration fee repealed in 2019).
states.insNote: Among the highest insurance costs in the nation — hail alley.

states.name: Kentucky
states.transfer: Deed tax of $0.50 per $500 (0.1%), paid by the seller.

states.name: Louisiana
states.transfer: No state real estate transfer tax (Orleans Parish charges a documentary tax).
states.taxNote: The homestead exemption shields the first $75,000 of value from most parish taxes.
states.insNote: Hurricane exposure has pushed premiums and Citizens-plan usage sharply higher.

states.name: Maine
states.transfer: Transfer tax of $2.20 per $500 (0.44%), split buyer/seller.

states.name: Maryland
states.transfer: 0.5% state transfer tax plus county transfer and recordation taxes (often 1.5%+ combined).

states.name: Massachusetts
states.transfer: Deed excise of $4.56 per $1,000 (0.456%) statewide.

states.name: Michigan
states.transfer: State transfer tax of 0.75% plus county 0.11%.
states.taxNote: Proposal A caps taxable-value growth at inflation or 5% (whichever is less) until the home sells — taxes 'pop up' to market value at transfer.

states.name: Minnesota
states.transfer: Deed tax of 0.33% (0.34% in Hennepin/Ramsey).

states.name: Mississippi
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.
states.taxNote: The lowest typical home value in the nation keeps dollar tax bills modest despite an average rate.
states.insNote: Gulf counties carry significant wind-coverage costs.

states.name: Missouri
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.

states.name: Montana
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.

states.name: Nebraska
states.transfer: Documentary stamp tax of $2.25 per $1,000 (0.225%).
states.insNote: Hail makes Nebraska one of the two or three most expensive states for home insurance.

states.name: Nevada
states.transfer: Real property transfer tax of $1.95–$2.55 per $500 (≈0.4–0.5%) by county.
states.taxNote: Annual tax-bill increases are capped at 3% for primary residences.

states.name: New Hampshire
states.transfer: Transfer tax of 1.5% total (0.75% each side) — high for the region.
states.taxNote: No state income or sales tax — property taxes carry the load, hence the fourth-highest rate nationally.

states.name: New Jersey
states.transfer: Graduated realty transfer fee ≈1% typical; extra 1% 'mansion tax' above $1M.
states.taxNote: The highest effective property tax rate in the nation — on the typical home that's over $11,000 a year, often more than insurance and PMI combined.

states.name: New Mexico
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.

states.name: New York
states.transfer: State transfer tax of 0.4% (plus 1% 'mansion tax' above $1M; NYC adds its own).
states.taxNote: Rates diverge sharply: upstate counties are among the highest-taxed in America while NYC's effective rate on 1-3 family homes is far lower.

states.name: North Carolina
states.transfer: Excise tax of $1 per $500 (0.2%); a few coastal counties add a land transfer tax.
states.insNote: Coastal wind exposure raises premiums east of I-95.

states.name: North Dakota
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.

states.name: Ohio
states.transfer: Conveyance fee of $1 per $1,000 (0.1%) state plus up to $3 per $1,000 county.

states.name: Oklahoma
states.transfer: Documentary stamp tax of $0.75 per $500 (0.15%).
states.insNote: The most expensive state for homeowners insurance — tornado and hail claims dominate.

states.name: Oregon
states.transfer: No transfer tax statewide (grandfathered exception: Washington County's 0.1%).
states.taxNote: Measure 50 caps assessed-value growth at 3%/year, so long-held homes are taxed well below market value.

states.name: Pennsylvania
states.transfer: 1% state realty transfer tax plus typically 1% local (Philadelphia ~3.28% local).

states.name: Rhode Island
states.transfer: Conveyance tax of $2.30 per $500 (0.46%).

states.name: South Carolina
states.transfer: Deed recording fee of $1.85 per $500 (0.37%).
states.taxNote: Owner-occupied homes are assessed at 4% versus 6% for other property — the primary-residence discount is unusually large.
states.insNote: Coastal wind pools apply along the shore.

states.name: South Dakota
states.transfer: Transfer fee of $0.50 per $500 (0.1%).
states.insNote: Hail exposure keeps premiums high for the region.

states.name: Tennessee
states.transfer: Realty transfer tax of $0.37 per $100 (0.37%).
states.taxNote: No state income tax and a below-average property tax rate make carrying costs low.

states.name: Texas
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.
states.taxNote: No state income tax — property taxes carry the load. The homestead exemption removes $100,000 of value from school taxes and caps appraisal growth at 10%/year.
states.insNote: Hurricane, hail, and freeze losses make Texas one of the priciest insurance markets.

states.name: Utah
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.
states.taxNote: Primary residences are taxed on only 55% of market value by statute.

states.name: Vermont
states.transfer: Property transfer tax of 1.25% (0.5% on the first $100k of a primary residence).
states.taxNote: Education property taxes are income-sensitized for many resident homeowners — the sticker rate overstates what lower-income households pay.

states.name: Virginia
states.transfer: State recordation tax of $0.25 per $100 (0.25%) plus grantor tax; localities add a third.

states.name: Washington
states.transfer: Graduated REET: 1.1% up to $525k, rising to 3% above $3.025M.
states.taxNote: No state income tax; the graduated real-estate excise tax hits high-price sales hardest.

states.name: West Virginia
states.transfer: Transfer tax of $1.10 per $500 state (0.22%) plus county $0.55+ per $500.
states.taxNote: The most affordable combination in the country: the lowest typical home value plus a below-average tax rate.

states.name: Wisconsin
states.transfer: Transfer fee of $3 per $1,000 (0.3%).

states.name: Wyoming
states.transfer: No real estate transfer tax.
states.taxNote: No state income tax and mineral revenues keep residential property taxes low.
